speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: I first think I was going to ask you about Grandfather O 'Malley, and I thought I'd ask you first off if you know how he happened to come to Idaho.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: I don't know very much about it. I know that he belonged to a family that had moved west and had settled around Athena and west in Oregon. Several of them came up here, according to family tradition at least, came up here to look over the location. The others were farmers, but he didn't care for that. He liked the cattle ranching. They came up here. Of course, you've heard all the stories about how the grass was chest high and all over the hills and so on. He thought this would be a wonderful place to raise cattle. He came up and first, I guess, was on a tract out near what I know as the Munson Farm out in the Robinson Lake area. It brought a herd of cattle up here. Then in one of the hard winters, what was it?I'm not sure.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: That was about 73 or 74.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Something like that. I think maybe that's about right. The cattle had heavy snows and the ground froze and the cattle couldn't get to the feed. He lost $40 ,000 worth of cattle, which was a goodly sum in those days. Apparently it was about that time that he decided to try something else. He was the store and that sort of thing. He was probably one of the main stockmen in the country, not the main one, when he came with that herd. That might be. I just don't know. You know, they say that in the history of North Idaho, he was the first man to settle in the Valley, Paradise Valley.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Do you know if that's so or not?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: I really don't. The summer of 1871. That was the time that my mother always said that the family came. I really don't know whether he was one of the early ones, certainly. At one time, he and Russell and McGregor and Deacon owned property that adjoined at Sixth and Main Street. That was the Four Corner Place. And he had the property west of Main and north of Sixth at that time. Now, I suppose that was a homestead that he took over from somebody.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Did he trade for, was it Neff that he got it from?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: This is what I've heard. I don't know. Whether he got all that land from Neff or not, or whether it was just the store. I thought from what mother had told me that it was the store he acquired from Neff, and the post office went along with it. But I don't know whether Neff had the homestead or not.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: But you think the reason that he went into town and started the store was because of that hard winter probably?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: I think so. That's what I've understood from my mother as the reason.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: And apparently he was very public -spirited and civic -minded and interested in developing the town and was busy in whatever was going on that way. I wonder if the town was just his store to start with. I think it was a little more than that. Oh, maybe to start with. But later on, the store was located at First and Main, where the Bank of Idaho is now. And White's Drugstore was across the way. You've seen those pictures with that. And Dr. McCauley had his dental office in that same block too, north of First Street. And I heard my mother tell about how she used to play with Margaret McCauley. They lived there within about a half -block of each other. And this is while my mother was still living there on First, before they built the house on Almond Street. Before that, she told me once they'd been in a house that was located where the old post office is now.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: When you had the store, did they live right by it, or was it a cabin?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Well, I had the impression that it was very close there. And my mother used to be in the store quite a bit, I guess, as a little girl. And she told me about how the Indian women would come in, and they'd be so fascinated with her red hair. They'd want to hold her and play with her because she had red hair.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: And she was pretty small at that time. Must have been.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Do you remember anything else that she said about what the store was like?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: No. She didn't say a great deal about what the store was like.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: This also was during the time when they had the Indian scare and they had Fort Russell, you know.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: And but I think she was only about three years old then, wasn't that, in 77?

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: That's what Homer David says too, when she was in the fort. Yes, well, she has told me about that, about how they would go, people in general would go and work their farms during the daytime and then go hole up in the fort at night.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: And she would go with her mother. Now, I don't know whether, whether...

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Oh, yes, by that time they must have been in town. I don't think they were on that farm any longer. And her mother would hitch up the wagon at the end of the day and they'd take the children up to the fort and they'd go in there and stay overnight. That didn't seem to last very long. So they spent their days on the farm, the women and children did too.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Does she remember at all what it was like in the fort or what she did in the fort?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Not very much. She didn't remember very much of that. She, somebody from Spokane wanted to come down and interview her about that and she discouraged him because she said she was just too young to have remembered very much about it. I think three or four is probably about the threshold of memory. No, she didn't seem to have very, very clear recollections of that and I certainly understand it.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: What about this question of the naming of Moscow, as you understand it?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: I've actually read in that history of North Idaho, I was looking at it the other day, they gave a well and the credit for deciding to name the town Moscow. Well, this seems to have been the object of considerable speculation and so on. I think grandfather probably was very influential, was right in the business of planning on that. The mother sort of changed her stories as the years went by. For years she told me that the reason they chose Moscow was that they wanted a name that no other towns in the United States had. Well, this, or maybe I misunderstood it, maybe they just didn't want one that was very close. Because there were, apparently, Neff had come from near Moscow, Pennsylvania.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Then later on, mother told me that they also wanted something that sounded citified and glamorous and that Moscow, Russia filled the bill as far as that was concerned.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Did she have any opinion about the question of Neff's naming the town after his hometown when she talked about it?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Well, I'm trying to remember.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: It seems the way she used to tell it as if it was more or less just a kind of, they reached a consensus on it. And of course, the men had wanted, they had called it hog heaven beforehand and the women objected to that because they thought that was not a very dignified name for a beautiful valley like this. So they discarded hog heaven and they wanted to call it

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Paradise, but I think the post office wouldn't approve that because there were other towns that had that same name.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: And she was very definite about the fact that it had nothing to do with Russians among the population. Somebody had written a book, as mother said, when he was old and senile and said that that was the reason that there were Russians that just would come down out of the hills occasionally and then disappear back into the hills and that they were responsible for naming it Moscow. And she was very positive about that fact that that wasn't true. And my father, when he was county auditor, did a good deal of checking with the county records. And he said there just were no Russian names around Moscow.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: And mother always felt that the reason that theory got accepted was because it appeared in print and that it wasn't reliable at all. I don't know why there's such controversy over naming Moscow, but there really seems to be. Well, and so many theories. I have a clipping someplace that somebody had given to mother and she just pooh -poohed the whole thing. It had to do with somebody pointing to my grandfather's beard and saying, you look like a Russian with that long beard.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Why don't we name it Moscow?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Mother thought that was perfectly ridiculous. There's nothing to that.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: You know, in this hog heaven, was that supposed to be because they ran hogs in the valley?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Hogs like to dig the camas has been my understanding of the tradition and grew very fat on the camas roots. And of course, no question that there was camas around here. And the men called it hog heaven because it was so good for the stock.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: What about the Indians that used to camp here in the early days? Did you ever hear about them and your mother?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Not very much. She used to mention the Indians coming into the store. And of course, she was aware of the fact that the Indian trails had run through here. But she didn't have very much to say about it. In fact, I can't remember her mentioning them particularly, except as in regard to her red hair. What about what Mr.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Wells sold mostly in the kind of business that he had in those days? You gleaned anything from looking at those ledgers that he had?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Well, yes, I think it was pretty general what you would expect of a country store. You know, tools that were commonly used and produce and staples.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: And apparently, he was taking produce in trade, you know, taking eggs and vegetables that people brought in and they got credit for. And so on.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Old fabrics and hats and shoes and just the whole gamut of what you'd supply if you were running a store in a little frontier town. And I read that he had to go to Walla Walla to get the first stock that he laid in. I'm curious about where he would have continued to get his stock after that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Why don't they have to keep going back to Walla Walla since it was before the railroad came in?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: He probably did. I don't remember hearing Mother talk about that very much. She did talk about how he banked up at St. John because there wasn't a bank here in Moscow. And he would ride horseback up to St. John. And when he was going was a rather carefully guarded secret because he'd be carrying gold and didn't want to get waylaid. But I don't remember that she ever said very much about where he got his stock.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Have you heard the story that the mail first came and was left deposited there in a shoebox in the store?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: I guess I've read that someplace. He started with a shoebox. Yes, I think I have. Of course, most of the early post offices were something similar to that. The mail would be left in somebody's home or in a business or something like that. That may well be true about the shoebox. And then he got out of the...

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: He sold his store and turned to selling land and platted the town of Moscow?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Yes, that was it. But he had financial reverses. Somebody absconded with money from a local bank and with a lot of records, a lot of things that had been left there for safekeeping, including a great many notes that my grandfather had taken for people who owed him money. And without the notes, he couldn't collect. So that, according to family tradition, he lost a good deal of money that way and was forced to make a choice between trying to maintain the store and trying to run the... Sell real estate and do that. And because he was so interested in building up the town, he chose to do that. And gave up the store.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Have you heard anything about this business of implating the town and then selling land?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: No, but I imagine that that is true because there's a... Lou Allen's edition is one of the sections of Moscow. And Mrs. Lou Allen's edition. And I think he was involved in the early plating of the town. Whether it extended to other parts of the town, besides what he owned, I don't know. I don't know, but I thought it said actually he platted the town from Moscow in 1880. He may have. What did...

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Did you tell me that there was something about him giving free lots to people?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: That's what my mother told me. That he would give lots free to people if they would build houses on them. This was just... This was a device for promoting the building up the town.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: And of course at that time, lots weren't very expensive anyhow. Lots down along Asbury and Lou Allen Street probably sold for $35. But he was interested in getting people to stay here and build homes for themselves.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Do you know anything about his political convictions?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: No, I don't. I think mother told me he was a Democrat. And having come from Tennessee, that could well be.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: But I don't know whether his convictions were very strong.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Was it he that told me that didn't care for his cherries, black Republican cherries?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: No, that was my other grandfather. That was my grandfather Woodworth, who was a very, very strong Democrat. And he had this cherry tree in his orchard, and he loved everything about the cherries except the name, black Republican.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Well, can you tell me overall what you understand your grandfather's being like? What kind of person he was that's come down through history anyway as the founding father of the town?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Only that he was just seemed to be mixed up in everything that was going on. Seemed to be involved in everything that was developmental or innovative in the town.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: But beyond that, I don't have any very strong impressions, because I never knew either one of my Llewellyn grandparents.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Are there other things that your mother told you about growing up in Moscow, and she was a young person that you remember?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: She went to Portland to school before there was a high school here. She went to St. Helens Hall in Portland, and I suppose she must have gone on the train.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: By that time, the railroad would have been in here, wouldn't it?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: She was born in 74. She'd be 11. And I don't know how many years she went down there, but I know that she did go down there to boarding school. And then when the high school was started here, she attended the high school. And I thought for years that she had graduated from high school here, but it turned out that she had married my father in January before she was due to graduate in May. So she didn't actually finish high school.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Oh, I think there was a lot of activity with the young people. That is, Mother belonged to a mandolin club, and she played the piano. And the mandolin club apparently had quite a membership. I have pictures of the members of the mandolin club, and they must have been old. Maybe 10, 12 mandolin players, and they used to play around for various social events. I think like any small town, they made their own fun.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Social life?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: No. No, in those days, married women didn't go to college. They didn't even go to college when I was in school. It was unusual for any married women to be in college. And the ones that were, we thought were sort of odd. I suppose when I was in college, I knew maybe half a dozen. And this was just...

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: What was, what would you think would be odd about it?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Well, married women stayed home and took care of their families.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: And it seems ridiculous now that it would be odd. But the one, even the ones that I knew in college mostly were wives of staff members who somehow or another hadn't completed their education. And I knew two or three that finished up here.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Single women though, it was completely acceptable for them to go to college on Mother's Day?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Oh, I don't know about that. Yes, yes, it was acceptable. But there just weren't very many of them that did. There weren't very many men that did either. And when they first started the university, they ran a preparatory school because the students that came were not really college, high school graduates, a lot of them. And there was a lot of talk when I was a child about the prep school. It was discontinued after a while when they started getting enough students that were that were prepared. But when it first started out, the prep school was an important part of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Was the prep school actually a high school?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: No, but I think it was sort of remedial courses for for those who hadn't finished high school and who needed more background.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: And people came from other parts of the state to go to that before going to the university?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: That's my understanding, that they do prep school work and then go on and finish their college. I don't know how long that went on, but it was a common term in the community.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Do you think they were taught by college teachers or had a separate faculty of their own? The prep school?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: I don't know.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: I just don't know about that.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Your uncle, that was your mother's brother, he went to college?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: He was enrolled, the one that was the athlete. Yes, he was a student in college, but not for very long because he died very young. But,

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: and he would have been the only one in my mother's family. She had a brother and sister who died very young.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Was that sickness?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Oh, I think it was probably the diphtheria epidemic.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: I don't know how old they lived to be. I'd have to look up the family records on that, but not very old. And she and this brother were the only ones that grew up at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: What did you hear about his prowess as an athlete?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Well, apparently he was a very good athlete. He was a baseball player and he was a track, very good in track. He got lots of medals in the track meets and so on.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: I doubt if he was around when they started having football. I don't know just when that was, but I don't think he was involved in that. But lots of baseball.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: I have medals that he won in intercollegiate track meets.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: You know, Mrs. Bush was one of Governor McConnell's daughters. And she lived up on the hill there, kind of back to back, yards back to back with the Homer Davids. And the Davids had four boys. And one time she was out in her backyard and she heard some of the David boys, I don't know which ones, talking on the other side of the fence. And they were saying, they were trying to say the dirtiest words that they knew. And one of them said, oh, I know a good one. Manure, manure. And after a while another one said, oh, I know a better one. Corset cover.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Now you remember that, Sam, when you want something really pornographic. Yeah, yeah. Boy. I think she must have been different for that to be the dirtiest thing they could think of.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: You know what corset covers were?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: It was a kind of camisole that women wore under their dresses. Well, it was the top to the petticoats, except the petticoats in those days were probably separate. And the corset covers would tuck in under them. Sometimes they were, oh, you know, they had lace and insertion and ribbon and that sort of thing. Yeah, I had to think about it. I really don't know what corset covers are. I mean, I figured what they'd be, but. Well, camisole is another name for it.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: But it's really going a long way to find a dirty word. Even manures.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Did you know the McCollodors?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Yeah, I knew Carrie. Well, slightly. I mean, they were of my mother's generation. I knew Carrie quite well because she lived here all her life, I guess, and was a friend of my mother's for.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: A long, long time and lived within a block of mother during during mother's last, oh, 15, 20 years that she lived here in town. The others, Mamie Bora and Ollie Ludaman, I met occasionally, but I didn't know them well at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Did you ever hear much about what the social life used to be like around the mansion when they were girls?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: I've heard that it was really quite something from over five. No, I don't think I ever heard very much about that. I guess maybe it was more that the McConnell daughters were sort of the center of Moscow society when they were young women. That may well be.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Okay, now what's the story about your brother Jack's run -in with the president of the university?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: My uncle. That's right, I mean your uncle. Yeah, well, according to mother, he had been competing in these track meets and he was he was one of the successful ones and the Idaho, University of Idaho had a track meet scheduled with Washington Agricultural College at Pullman and the rivalry was quite strong and Idaho had been beating the ag college and President Galt called my uncle in and said that in the interest of good relations between the two colleges, he didn't want the University of Idaho to win so big and so he wanted my uncle to see that he didn't win too many events. And my uncle said he didn't play that way. He played to win and he would feel that he had to do his best and win as win or lose as he could. And President Galt said that if he disobeyed him, he would expel him from the country. And he called, according to mother's story, he called my grandfather and told him that if Jack didn't agree to do this, that he would expel him from school. And Jack refused to agree to do it and he did expel him from school.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: You think Jack got to run the races?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: I don't think he got to take part in that meet. I could be wrong about that, but I think he was not allowed to compete.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: That's something that wouldn't get very far these days either. His father would probably be down there right now talking to the president of the university. Probably. But in those days, of course, administrators had a good deal more power over individuals. Than they do now. That is, it was a kind of dictatorship. You either shaped up or you couldn't stay in school.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: I'm really curious about what the university was like back in the early days. I guess it didn't work for very many students at all, probably when your uncle was going there. I don't imagine so. I don't imagine there would have been very many.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: For many years, my mother knew all the students at the university. Because there were so few of them and they were fairly well integrated into the life of the town apparently.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Being in musical circles, as she was, she just knew everybody. Out of the first few graduating classes, I don't imagine there'd be anybody she didn't know.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: There were some events then where they'd get together for music and that sort of thing. Oh, I think so. I think that was quite common.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Do you know how she met your father?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: No, I really don't. She was very young. That is, she must have been... Well, she was not quite 18 when she was married. And I don't know how long she had known Dad. But he probably was working here in town. And the town was probably small enough that everybody knew everybody else.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: I don't know beyond that.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: And then after they got married, they stayed here for some time?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Yes, they lived here for quite some time. Then I think father went up to Wallace. And they lived there for a little while. And then they lived in Lewiston for a short time, when my brother was a baby, when he was very young. And then came back here.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Dad was kind of the auditor at one time, I don't know exactly when. And he'd been in the abstract business. I think that's what he was doing in Wallace and also in Lewiston.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: And then they lived here. I was born here apparently. By that time they were settled down here.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: And then your father had this sort of wanderlust for farming?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Yeah, I think he was a frustrated farmer because he went up to Canada

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: later on and farmed a big ranch up there for Dr. Gritman. Dr. Gritman was, I guess, was making quite a bit of money and wanted to put it to work. And this was one of his ventures, was this farming in Canada. And he had quite a big ranch up there near Champion in Alberta. Dad went up there several years and farmed for him. I suppose by that time I was in high school. Maybe not. Yeah, probably. Probably I was in high school by that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Well, so he would go up and farm part of the year and then come back down?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Yes. Yeah, that was it. And then later he farmed for himself in Saskatchewan for several seasons.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: I think that he sold out up in there about 1926. As I remember, that was about the time, 25 or 26. It's funny, his family seemed to have moved around a lot too, as I read some of the materials that he dictated to you. They seemed to have come from a lot of places. Yes, his father had farmed near Genesee and then he had a store at Uniontown. And then he had a farm down on the breaks of the snake near Uniontown. Then moved back to Moscow.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: And he had come from Montana in 1876. They had moved from Montana, they went down through O 'By Henry's Lake and down through South Idaho and around and up to get to Moscow, to get to this part of the country.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: So that was quite a little trek in itself.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Were you here when you were a little kid in Moscow for most of your growing up?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Except for the couple of years that we spent in California. I spent all my life here until I was out of college.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Would you tell me about that trip that when you went down to California and New Mexico?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Well, we went to California because of my mother's health. And while he was down there, somebody sold him on the idea that he could, I suppose, homestead and raise wheat in New Mexico. So we all went off to a little place called Playas in New Mexico, which was right down near the Mexican border.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: And it was just, oh, such a very small place. There wasn't anything there but a store. I don't even remember a gas station. Maybe there was one. But the main line of the Southern Pacific came through there. It didn't stop at Playas unless you telegraphed ahead that you were going to flag it. And then it would stop and pick up passengers, or it would let off paying passengers. But it picked up the mail by hooking it off a pole and tossed off mailbags. And we were down there only a few months because it was at the time of the Mexican border trouble. And the week that we arrived there, the the Postal Service had held some kind of meeting of postmasters in that area. And they had gone into a town called Columbus, New Mexico. And Pancho Villa's bandits had crossed the border and raided the town and had had taken some of the some of the convention, conventioneering postmasters out and lined them up and shot them. And this was all the talk of just about the time we arrived. And the postmaster from Playas had been there and was killed.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: And then later, after what I think probably was three or four months only, we left there very hurriedly because someone came came riding into our yard one day and said that a young fellow and his wife who were ranching about, oh, I think maybe it was about 30 miles away from us, had been killed by their Mexican farmhands who had then taken off across the border and into Mexico. And my mother decided that was that was no place for her children. So we left. We left the very next day and and father left in about a week or two. So that was the end of that.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: But that was of course, this that was much earlier than then father was in Canada, because this was before this was about the time I was starting to school. I think I had been part of a year to school in Los Angeles before this. And then we went back to California and I went to first grade again there. But he decided to come back from California, too. The reason that he decided to come back from California was that the house on Almon Street that Grandfather Llewellyn had built had been rented while we were in California to the Kappa Sigma fraternity for a fraternity house. And while we were gone, they decided to build their own house on the campus. And so Dad, in the meantime, had gotten the idea from observing apartment houses in California. He had decided that he could convert that house on Almon Street into an apartment house. So when the when the Kappa Six gave it up, we came back here and Dad spent. Oh, what must have been six months or so anyhow, remodeling it into apartments. And that was that was really the first real apartment house in Moscow. It was the first I think the first one to have electric ranges and and to have shower baths and folding beds, beds that folded down out of the wall. And this was this was quite the thing. The the waiting list to get apartments there. We all there was always a waiting list in the in the early times up until the time of the Depression.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: But who were the people that wanted to stay there?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Oh, we had bankers and physicians and university professors. It was.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Was this supposed to be the new the newest thing in living?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Well, yes, it was innovative. This was just this is just something new from Moscow. When you mentioned to me, you mentioned this to me before about the built in kitchen that he had and there was a there were some new besides the stove. Well, the kitchen cabinet and this was a combination cupboard and it was a freestanding piece of furniture. It wasn't really built in, but it was a combination of cupboard and breakfast table. It was a compact sort of unit. There was a place for supplies and and dishes. And then the the counter pulled out to make to make a place that you could eat, you know, like a breakfast table. And I had had a place for a flower.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Built in flower storage place with a sister right at the bottom so that you could load the load a sack of flour into it and then sift it right on out of there. You didn't have to scoop it out.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: And it had an old bread drawer, silverware drawer, all this sort of thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Did he do the remodeling himself on these places?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Oh, he did quite a bit of it. He hired carpenters and and plumbers. He was he was quite good at that sort of thing. He was good with his hands and and I'm sure that he did the the designing of it, but he hired carpenters to work on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Do you have anything what kind of rent he was getting in those days?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Oh, I suppose

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: I think the most expensive apartment probably was the other ground floor apartment. We lived on the ground floor and there was one other ground floor apartment and I think that rented for fifty dollars. And I'm sure that was the most expensive one.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: You know there's a story about your mother that I remember, just remembered you telling before. That was about her playing with the gold. Oh, yes, about playing with the gold piece, the sack of twenty dollar gold pieces. She didn't have very many toys, I suppose, and so at least once, maybe only once, she was given this sack of twenty dollar gold pieces to play with. At the end of the day, when they checked up, they found that one was missing and they asked her about it and she said yes. And she took them out and showed them where she had planted it. She knew about planting seeds and growing more and so she planted this twenty dollar gold piece in the hope of getting more. So they dug up the twenty dollar gold piece.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: I suppose you know that the streets, Almon, Asbury, and Llewellyn are named for my grandfather. That was his name, Almon, Asbury, Llewellyn. And Lily is named for my mother, but through some error, it was supposed, they were supposed to be, the streets were supposed to be in order. Almon, Asbury, Llewellyn, and then Lily, but somehow in drawing up the the plat, they got them in the wrong order. And so they run Almon, Asbury, Lily, and then Llewellyn, in that order. Well, it's not too bad. It's Almon, Asbury, and Lily, Llewellyn. Actually quite nice. Yes, I thought that was kind of interesting.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Do you know anything about Noel Llewellyn?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: No, I don't, I don't, not at anything like first hand. I know that he was a school teacher here and that he was probably related to mother. He probably was a, could have been an uncle. I just don't know. There was a, there was a big family of the Llewellyns down around Athena and West, and then quite a few of them came up here too. And I know that there were motherhead cousins and

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Llewellyn cousins and so on, but I just don't know about Noah Llewellyn.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: What about how your father got to know so many of the the old timers and the the old happenings?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Like he seemed to know from what you told me quite a bit about how the university got started. He personally knew Willis Sweet and those people. Well, I think that he was just, he was just here early. He and his family must have come in in about 76 or seven, and he grew up in in the area and then became a young, young businessman and interested in politics and so on. I think he just, just knew everybody that there was locally.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: And he, he knew Willis Sweet and he knew, oh,

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: a lot of, a lot of the, of the early, the prominent early men like that, but after all, Moscow was probably, was pretty small. And I think that, that a man in business like my father probably did know all the other, all the other businessmen, men and all the politicians.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: He told me he was a staunch Republican?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: He was raised a Democrat. He was raised by the grandfather that didn't like the source of sorrow to my grandfather when dad turned out to be a backsliding Democrat. And when he converted from being a Democrat to a Republican, he became one of the most reactionary conservative Republicans that you could hope to find anywhere. They used to tease him about belonging to the I asked my mother at one time if there ever was a president at either party that, that dad approved of and she said no, that none of them suited him. They all did, did most things wrong. Now he was really very, very conservative.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Once he quit being a Democrat.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: What would that mean? I mean, can you think of any, of any particulars that, that he would feel that he would felt very conservative about?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Well, I think he was kind of all for big business. He felt that, that, that business was what had built this country and that, that this was, businessmen deserved quite a bit of credit and consideration and so on. And he used to get quite upset when my brother and I were, would advance the idea that we didn't think that, that business was that much

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: entitled to special privileges and so on.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: He just didn't, he just was not a man who had liberal ideas. Well, I guess you and your brother picked up, picked up some when you were young, those ideas that business wasn't entitled to special privilege were, were ideas that, that you absorbed. Well, yes, I think that we, maybe when, when we grew up and got away from father's influence and had a little chance to look around for ourselves, that we, that we saw things a little bit differently. Maybe it's just, it's a generation gap, but.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Well, if he hated all presidents, you think that was mostly because he didn't like the federal government?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: No, I don't think he hated them all, but I think he, he just felt that they could have done things differently. And

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: I think he probably, he probably felt that Hoover was, was one of the better presidents that we had.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: And he disliked Roosevelt very much. He just thought Roosevelt was terrible. It's funny, from what I've heard, it seems like a number of Democrats became Republicans during Cleveland's time. I think that 1893 depression, that might have been quite a few. That might've had something to do with it. I never did know what it was particularly that caused him to, to change.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: There are the thoughts about this country, how great it is. Well, we went, we got out of food, the making difference. And then we got to Spokane and mother said, I don't know whether she said that the dime that I first gave her or something, but anyway, she gave me a dime.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: She said, did you say you wanted to have something, buy something for that dime?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Yes. I said, I do. And so I went by myself. I went out and I, I thought the conductor said that be ready to board the train at eight o 'clock. Well, like clock, clock, clock, clock, is Swedish, you know, I knew that and many other words too. And then

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: I looked in the window and I thought, Oh, what am I going to do with this dime? And I thought, if I could only find a little book, I had the idea in my head that if a book was small, why would be more easy to read? It's not pamphlet, you know?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: And, and I asked how much that was.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: And he looked at me and he said, what are you going to use it for?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Well, I want that little book. If, if this is enough. Well, I think you can get it for less than 10, five cents. He said, maybe. And, and then he said, well, well, keep, keep that five.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: He said, and then what you're going to do with the five now?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Well, I said, mother is sick and, and, and she can't do anything about it. And I thought if we could get a few cherries, each one of us gave me bag of cherries. Then I was on top of the world, I tell you. Then onto the train again, you know, so Spokane and, Oh, we fixed our hair, you know, and we fixed up in every way and, and check the baggage and everything. So it wouldn't lose anything. And by the way, when we crossed the planes, we had hats, mind you, we got hats because we're going to America and we had them on a shelf above us, you know, likely to have all kinds of things. And the, those tornadoes things, twisters, you know, on the planes, you know, Kansas and Nebraska and those places. And all the hats, but one went out through that, through that. Well, mother didn't have hers on. It was, I don't know, well, maybe it's only three that, that went through. But I didn't make any difference. We're getting home, getting home. First home that we've ever had. It's been like anything now, going home, going to the United States. Oh my, my, my. And then, right out here and there stood my father at the bottom of the steps. And mother started out first and, and Signe, my sister, eight year sister, seven, I guess she was, somehow I kicked her because I jumped right in my father's arms. And then a few feet away from it against the, the, the,

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: what is it for the trains go by down here?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Depot. Depot. And there stood Uncle Storm. Oh, gray haired, you know, white, you know, and holding out his arms too. And then we walked to the hotel, the same hotel that is there now. And they had, Thompson was the name of the man that owned that hotel at that time. And they had a special dinner for us because Gabe, my father, has told him that his family was coming and we didn't get in before about two o 'clock in the afternoon on a Sunday. And oh my, the food, the meat, all those, in those days they had platters for each, they don't have, they didn't have plates for ordinary plates, but they had, the meat part was just like it is now when we have the two pass, the meat platter, you know.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: And then how far is it to, to, to go?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Oh, just about another hour now, maybe, driving and, and there was a man here, he was only about 10, 12 years old, and father got the livery barn, you know, hired a rig at the livery barn that boy drove, you know. And I can't, can't find him, I can't, I'm not so sure of the name or anything. Anyway, he dumped us and went back and then we were home.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: What do you suppose we did?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Well, we cried for one thing and we thanked God for another one and I've always done that. And father has gotten some, I have a plate here that belongs to my mother. And you sit down, this is my chair, there's a reason for it. I kept two, two room that he had finished. He was a carpenter, he, he built, he built, built it of logs.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Hadn't I told you this?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Built it of logs and had a broad access, one of the first things he bought, a broad access, you know, you, you, you can walk on the ties and things like that and hew them like that. Not long ago, you know, that all the ties were hewed with scored.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: You score the log, you cut it a V in it like this. So the next log can, you put that, you score it on one side only and then you put the other one on the scored on top. So it fits like this, you see. And then they, they have a braid, not brace and bits, but the bigger ones that you bore a hole in, in, through both of them. Here are the two logs now, here's one log and another log. And then you have a wooden peg about that wide, that wide, a couple of inches. And, and the hole is just a little bit, it has to fit of course. And then into the other one like that. So all over our building, our, our, our pegs like that is put together by pegs, no nails. Wasn't a single nail when we came.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: It was all like that with the exception of, let's see now, what was that now?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Well, the corn, he had this one, Erickson, Asween, who lived here, he died several years ago, but, but he, this,

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: oh, Thomassons, close to the cemetery, owns a place now that he made for himself, this, this man. And he, he, the rest of them have followed suit. And, and the corners, they prayed that the time would come, it slipped. So father and that man had a brace, had went, had a place to make, to work with metal, you know, heavy metal, plowshair or something had to be fixed, you know, and, and harness go to pieces and so on. Then they go to Sven -Eriksen and to fix it. And so the corner of that house is a thing like this. And then there's nails on the side, iron nails, but they were square nails, you see, and they wouldn't stay there as long as I stay around here. Oh, I tell you, I, I, I'm sick because I ever came here to this cabin.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: What did you think, what did you think of the, of the cabin when you got there, of the homestead?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Oh, you can't imagine for people that haven't had anything, you know, and come to a place like that and they smelled of cedars so nicely, you know, and father had, had, he had varnished the woodwork and instead of having paper of some kind on the wall, on the walls, he had oilcloth, you know, and putting the strips like that. So it's artistic too, you know, it was nice. And then as time went on, why we had the whole room, 16 by something, the, of the, one end of the house was never finished. The, the openings for the, for the windows was, was there a long time, didn't board over, you know, and then slept in the, and in one room upstairs was finished shortly after we got home, got there. And then we had the bed in the kitchen and we had a bed in the, in the bedroom, but the bedroom was, was finished, you see. So we got along very well and it didn't take long before Al began to go to work, you know. We were going to school, Olivia was 16 and she, father told her that I fed you long enough, now you go on and earn your own living. He is, he's a wonderful, was a wonderful man, but why you don't fool with men like that. He thought that he, she should and she did too. And just as soon as she could, why the two of them, you know, trained for nurses for three years, four or whatever, like that, Great Falls, Montana is quite an institution there. I like to go there sometime. I had been there once, Catholic. And the years went by, we walked to Spring Valley School, four miles, and had, here is the bucket that we carried, you know, somebody, my father didn't smoke tobacco, but every Saturday morning he went to Troy, drove to Troy. He shaved and he bathed and he dressed up in work clothes, of course, don't have much of anything but that. He went to Troy and, and played pool with the Driscoll boys. Well, he's a good sport too. The older I got, the more I appreciated him too, because he worked, I tell you. Well, anyway, we landed in Troy the 3rd of August. And my father and another Swede from the locality from which we had come, uh, were working on a church in Deary, the Deary church. They've moved it since. And, and he said, now he said, you're going to be alone tomorrow because I, I have to finish the tower on that church and clean up with things and so on. So it wouldn't take long, but he'll be there a few days. And this organ has been in that church.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: And, and, let me see now. He said to mother, now you see too that they, they work. There were thistle. He has planted, had planted a garden and thistles had grown up all along them. And, uh, uh, as Scandinavian skin is very thin in a country like that, they talk about, uh, the peach, it glow or something like that, but it thin and, and, uh, you easily tear the skin, you know, because they are not used to such a much, so much wind like this and, and so much heat in, uh, summertime here, you know. So, uh, he told, he told him, but look out, but don't, don't put something on your head so that you won't get burnt. Oh, we didn't have anything to put on. And, uh, see now then Esther and I went to spring valley three years and, uh, the teacher that we had, the first one that we had, John Ogden, the first two years, and he was a terrible thing. If a boy snickered or, or, or he looked back so he saw it, he had to pocket knock and he threw it and pinned it. He was, had to do working on a piece of paper, you know, and he pinned that paper to the desk.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Should a man be beheaded for that?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Scared him, scared him all.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Well, anyway, when that was over and during that time, father couldn't have accumulated much money, you know, couldn't, you know, because he was still in trading things in this country and he, uh, see now, how was that now?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Now I, I skipped, I, I, uh, I read every night and I had a telekendel. We have made telekendels until just lately and so, and all those things, you know, it was very little that we bought because he got a cow and the calf was a male calf and so butchered him and then we got a pig and those days why they aren't, weren't so very much. I think somebody got one for a dollar, a baby pig, you know, that was just weaned. But as time went on, I, I, as I said, I, I wanted to study all the time and the first book when I got into the Spring Valley School, I had read, now that, that book is very common one.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Now what, what would you, what would the name of a book on a shelf in a schoolhouse of a conglomeration of people, Norwegians and Swedes and Irish and is from the Missouri and all over now, what kind of a book would, would you, would you want to read?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Especially if you read it before.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Uncle Tom's Cabin. I had, I read that in Swedish, you know, it's translated into many languages, most good books are, you know, and so there was, Uncle Tom's Cabin and I, I read and I could translate it so quickly to the others because, you see, I had the book, Uncle Tom's Cabin, you see, and there is a word here and there that is exactly the same, like we say book, B -O -O -K in Sweden, B -O -K book, B -O -K book, B -O -O -K book and water, vaten, V -A -T -E -N -W -A -T -E -R is on it, that's only the difference. It's, it's, it's, it's not as I said, not as hard as, but few people would, would bother with anything like that, the older ones, you know, they, they had hard times, that's all there's to that. I know people here in this town, they never may speak of it, just as if it would be a disgrace, I don't, I'm proud, I'm proud of the country that we've come to.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Well, anyway, we, from there on then, I, I worked out one summer here on the ridge and I hadn't had time to be confirmed when I first got here because I, I was, I had to work, you see, and so I went to, to confirmation school here, I'm not a Catholic, I'm Lutheran, and like, I don't see any difference in them myself, Lutherans or Catholic or all kinds of, I, I have often wondered why they have to be so many different kinds, you know, under one God and, and to, to, that we can't agree, for some concern, it doesn't make any difference.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: I think they think I'm a heathen, I know, but, but, but I'm not.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: I, my sister Esther easily cried when she was a child and one time she had spilled something and she says, oh dear God, why did you let me spill that milk? And I was astonished and say, you're blaming God for me?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: But I had to laugh, that was funny, you know, she was just kind of scared, you know, to, to, oh, I think, I think that all of us would get to the right place, I don't, I don't, I don't worry about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Where was the Spring Valley School? Well, when you leave Troy here and go to Deary, you have gone that way, well, the, the first main road that, what did you ask me?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Spring Valley. Spring Valley Schoolhouse, the first big road to the left, coming from Troy, and the first road to the left was in a corner, the schoolhouse was in a corner, Spring Valley School.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: The main road went then, as it does now, towards Deary, and then here the road going towards Deary, and this one is the one that takes off, and that, that divides here to go to the Clifton, and this one to that Spring Valley Lake, and then it comes to the same, to our place, at the end of it, when it comes together, and what else about White Bank?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: That was all I asked. Oh yeah, well, they, they moved that and made a garage, I think, I think that the Harry, Harry Glacier has bought that. Oh yeah, that was an awful road then, when we first came, it was terrible. Goes, walks so far, and often we used to have salt, and some lard, and you set for butter if we didn't have anything else, but I wasn't alone. When I was in Sweden, I, I could remember many times I, I was proud, you know, and we set a school room, and the room where we had called it a resting room or something like that, they settled benches all around like this, oh, a big room, because they had, they had many children, that's in Sweden, and the, they'd eat, and they'd have apples, and they'd have cotton candy, there were those, I could, that, have things then that, didn't have it, and then, so that they wouldn't make fun of me, or, or realize that I didn't chew anything, I just chewed with my hand, my hand, you know, and pretended to.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: But it has gone fine. Then, then I, I went to Pullman, and Dr. E. A.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Bryan, the president of the college, have I told you this?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Olivia was happen, happened to get their place to work for Mrs. Bryan, and I had worked for, up in the American region, got enough to get a ticket to Pullman, so we, we met, we, we met Dr. Bryan, too, and they were very nice to us, and she told me afterwards, Olivia stayed there, I think almost a year before she went to, to Great Falls, Montana, and, but Dr. Bryan said, don't you ever stop studying, don't ever stop, because he knew that had books with me in my suitcase when I came and everything, I had to, I had to go all the time.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: One time he went to, he, he also was a Sunday school teacher, he, I don't know what kind of a religion they had, he was a wonderful man, though, and he, I was studying, and he went through the room, the dining room, and he says, what are you reading?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: I said, my lessons.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Who, who, where are you going to school?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: I'm not going to school right now.

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Well, what are you studying then?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: Well, I can read. I said, I, I know what I'm doing, but because you, that's Latin, you can't, you can't have had that, I certainly have, I said, and that one was in Sweden. I had Latin and, and geometry when I was 12 years old, but I don't remember much of it now, I think I did, if I wanted to, but I, that's why I get so disgusted with some kind of kids, they, they, they just can't appreciate what they have. The, the books that they have, the art that they see, just kick at things and throw it away, and oh, that's nothing. That's wrong, it's wrong, parents have to train them differently. I blame them more than anything else.

speaker: Sam Schrager or Laura Schrager
words: Did you have a hard time learning English?

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: No, no, nothing. There were so many Scandinavians in this part of the country at that time, and Irishmen, you know, Irishmen, Scandinavians, and, and there, when they built the railroad down here in 1892, I think, four or something like that, six, they, they,

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: they, they are Italians, yeah, they Italians, more Italians though on that railroad than anyone else. Swedes went to the woods and, and, and piled homesteads, you see, have homes, have homes, and they, there was a story about the name of Troy. Well, now then, they're, let me find some papers someday, it's too long, but, but they, they wanted to have a name for Troy. Well, I know that the first, this come down from Moscow, Huff's Gulch, a fellow by the name of Huff filed for a quarter section or something like that, but I don't think it was first, because before that, before I knew about that, why people talked about, oh, run down to the, run down to the hotel, or run down to the store and get the mail, because they had, the mail went as far as Troy, had come then as far as Troy, you know, or Moscow, they go horseback to Moscow to get the mail, and, and the,

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: how was that now, now they, they,

speaker: Lillian Woodworth Otness
words: these Italians and the Swedes, the Irish, the Irish especially, fought with different people, you know, what is the word I want, that, you were Swedish, nationality, yes, nationality, and so it happens that they had a fellow by the name of Vollmer came in here, he was rich, and he staked so many people out in Genesee and all over, you know, and then he just skunked them, he just was almost driven out of the country, but he was a rich man. Well, anyway, they, then they, they had talked, been talking about the, what would be the name, it had been Vollmer, Swedes said it was Vollmer because they didn't, couldn't say the V very easily, and then this, this day, they were standing in the main street, nothing but stumps and sawdust in Troy then, you know, great big cedar stumps, you know, and they go with their rings, you know, wagons, you know, around, you know.
